headline: Custer Creek Train Wreck, 1938
body:
The night of June 19, 1938, was a black maw swallowing the Montana plains. Rain, a solid sheet of it, hammered down, turning dry coulees into raging torrents. Custer Creek, usually a trickle, swelled into a beast, a churning, muddy monster thirty feet high. Upstream, a bridge, AA-438, stood, a skeletal defiance against the rising fury. Built in 1913, its concrete piers and steel girders had seen many seasons, but none like this. The earth beneath it, softened by four to seven inches of relentless downpour, began to give way. The water, a relentless hammer, scoured at the foundations, gnawing away at the very bedrock. 

On the rails, the Milwaukee Road's Olympian, a westbound titan of steel and steam, thundered through the deluge. Class S-2 4-8-4 No. 220, a mechanical heart pumping power, pulled eleven cars, carrying 155 souls from Chicago to Tacoma. Fifty miles an hour, a blur in the dark, the engineer, a man of iron nerves and practiced hand, saw no warning. No water on the tracks, no sign of the abyss that waited. The bridge, though undermined, held its shape, a deadly illusion. 

Then, at 12:35 a.m., the moment arrived. The locomotive, then seven passenger cars, plunged into the roaring maw of Custer Creek. A chaos of crumpled steel, splintered wood, and shattered glass. On the west bank, the locomotive and five cars piled into a grotesque monument of destruction. The engineer, a testament to duty, was found still in his seat, hand on the throttle, even in death. Two other cars vanished into the frigid, churning depths. 

Rescue efforts began in the immediate aftermath, a desperate struggle against the elements. Train crew and uninjured passengers, their faces grim in the flickering light, smashed windows of submerged cars, creating escape routes from the watery tomb. But the toll was heavy. Forty-nine lives were claimed by the creek, though the official count stood at 47, for some bodies were swept downstream, swallowed by the Yellowstone River, one found fifty miles away in Glendive. Seventy-five more bore the marks of the wreck, injured, scarred. The modern comforts of the Olympian, its sealed windows and shatterproof glass, became a cruel trap, contributing to the grim tally. The investigation, a cold, hard look at the facts, concluded that the water's "volume and velocity... was much in excess of any that had been experienced before or might be anticipated at this place." The bridge, they found, was intact when the train reached it, but two central piers had been undermined. The weight of the locomotive, a final, crushing blow, caused the piers to subside, and the bridge to collapse. The night had claimed its victims, a stark reminder of nature's raw, untamed power.
